Hey guys, all right, so welcome to this video in the training series. What we're going to be going over is ways to mitigate the risk of having your ad account, your ads or any of your business manager accounts disabled or deactivated by Facebook. Now keep in mind that there's no issue with our ads, our copy, our funnels. These are just precautionary steps to make sure that we comply with Facebook's guidelines and if for whatever reason your ad account gets disabled, we don't want you to just be unable to generate leads. So we have other options for you. That being said, keep in mind that the Facebook policy for advertising is on their website. You can look at it at any point in time and it's constantly being updated. So if you run into ad accounts or ads that are declined or disabled, just go through the Facebook policy guidelines and try to match it up like side by side with your ad to see which one you possibly could have voided. And that usually helps out to understand moving forward. So some of the things that we've noticed as precautionary measures, so having the word free in the ad or in the funnel, obviously in the funnel, that means anything anywhere in ClickFunnels, whether it's the domain or any text inside the funnel, you don't want it to say free in the ad. Of course that's going to be, you can see we're in the campaign ad set and then I created a sample ad right here. So that would mean anywhere right here in the primary text in the website URL, basically you don't want the word free anywhere on the ad. Now placing links in the copy, just use the button. So what that means is right here, you could see the website URL 123fitness.com slash opt-in, that's where you want to keep it. So you don't want your ad to say, hey, we're local Jim and you know, you make your offer and you say, click here to get in and boom, like you don't want to do that. You don't want to have that link in a different spot other than that website URL field. So we're going to erase that and then that will be left there. Having before and afters in the funnel number three. So Facebook doesn't like you when you put before and afters of other people for one reason or another. Avoid that at all costs, even if it's on the thank you page, the opt-in page, whatever it is, do not have any before and afters in your funnel for sure not in your advertisement itself, not placing a privacy policy in your funnel. So you want to avoid that you want to have a privacy policy at the bottom of your funnel. Most of them should be down at the bottom. It says privacy policy, you click it and it goes to a link that shows your privacy policy. Ideally you have your own that you've already drafted with the lawyer as a business should have a privacy policy, especially if you're running any sort of ads. But if you don't get that drafted and then work with our team to get it onto your funnel. So we do have examples that you can go off of, but it is recommended you consult with your own legal attorneys because everything is different state by state, city by city, country by country. Now moving forward to number five, make sure to customize your funnel with your own images. What that means is that if you have a funnel that you're using that a hundred other people are using, it may it raises red flag for Facebook and you don't want to have the same exact images. Now you have the same funnel style, but you're using your own images, you're automatically out of that pool because Facebook uses like facial facial recognition technology and the same technology to identify what an image is and compare it to other images. It knows when there's a thousand funnels that are using the same image. So it tends to flag those. We want to make sure that all your images in your funnel and your creative, all that are original as possible. It's fine if you don't have them just to start off, but you want to make sure that you do get them. Like if it's an image of your facility or if it's an image of your staff, whatever it is, try to make it your own. And it also breeds that brand consistency with people who see your ad and they come into your gym and then they see a familiar face that they saw in the ad. So that's always cool. Unneeded redirects. If you don't know what a redirect is, you probably don't have to worry about this, but if you have redirected your landing page or your domain into anything, like if you have a redirect that goes into your funnel, you want to avoid that because any kind of redirect raises a flag for Facebook and it can be viewed as like a scrupulous method to get somebody to do something else other than what you're advertising for. So Facebook keeps an eye out on that. So if you have a redirect set up, it's recommended that you do not use it. Actually use your direct funnel link. Add that focus on specific body parts. So we used to do a big booty boot camp, for example, and that got flagged and we realized it was because we're focusing on the big booty and Facebook doesn't like that. If you're focusing on like belly fat or if you're focusing on someone's belly, if you're focusing on someone's chest and your copy and your ad creative and anything, try to avoid that. Do not do that because Facebook does not like it when you zone in on one exact thing. So moving forward from that, some things to keep note of. If you're brand new to Facebook advertising, you don't want to pour all your money in even if you can afford $500 a day and ads, you don't want to do that right off the bat. Let's say you have 10 ads and they're at $50 a day. That's way too much for just one day, especially for starting off and you don't know if the ads are going to be good or not. What you want to do is you want to have like, let's say those same 10 ads, start them off at $10, $15 a day and then from there, figure out which ones are working the best and scale up from 15 to 20 or 10 to 15 and then you're shut off the ones that are not working the best. So you want to have them from there, start moving it up $5 a day, $10 a day and slowly until you have the best performing ad and it's optimized to its full potential. So another thing to keep note of is keeping load times down on landing pages. So what that means is when somebody clicks your ad, how long does it take for them to actually see your page? I'm sure we've all been there where we waited for something to load, it didn't load within five seconds and you're out. That's how people view everything in Facebook and keep in mind they're all viewing it mobile too. So if they don't have the best service, that's a double way me with your page loading slow. One way to avoid this is not having huge videos or files directly embedded into the page, instead link to them and send them to an outside resource from that link. So if you have like a YouTube video, don't embed it in the funnel, send, you know, have the link that sends them to YouTube to view the video. That's one major, major way to decrease the load time. Now how to prepare in the event that you do actually have your ad account gets disabled or your business manager or your ads keep getting declined. What you want to do to prepare for that is have a second ad account because none of these things guarantees that Facebook won't disable it. These are just precautionary methods. So have that second ad account in place that uses different credentials, different business manager account, different personal account, different everything basically, and different payment method also. So you want to make sure that nothing ties back to the other ad account that you have and you want to make this ad account not when your other ad account goes bad, but before it because you want to have it prepared and ready since it can be an issue if you try to make another ad account later on after your ad account has troubles. So the next thing you want to do is after you create that second ad account, make sure to have run traffic on it at least, you know, once a week, I would say. So what I would do is set a budget of $20 to $50 for the week, run a couple ads on that account and just have them continue continually updating and make that part of your process when you are optimizing your ads to just check that account. See how the ads are doing in there once a week, twice a week, three times a week, however often you decide to do it, but just make sure that it's something you can afford and minimum, I would say is $20 a week to spend on it just to keep it active and keep it in good standing because you don't want your second ad account to get disabled for not being active. So that's the point right there. Now, also, if you are in a situation where you don't have any other ad accounts and you don't have any other methods to generate leads, use the cold messenger training for now while you're trying to get everything back up. If you don't know where that's at, message into the chat box and they should be able to guide you. And if you do know where that's at, go to it, implement it. You should be doing it regardless, but especially in this situation to generate leads. Otherwise, thank you all for watching this video and for your time and I will see you later either in a text support call or a data call.
